subtitle: Microbes Are a Menace!
summary: In 1831, microbe hunting has stalled while the rest of science races ahead. Nine-year-old Louis Pasteur witnesses a horrifying scene in his French village: a man cauterized with a red-hot iron after being mauled by a mad wolf, and eight victims die of hydrophobia. The traumatized boy grows up as an artistic, plodding student who slowly develops fierce ambition, eventually falling in love with chemistry at the Normal School in Paris. Meanwhile, two overlooked researchers — Cagniard de la Tour and Schwann — quietly prove that yeasts are alive and that microbes cause putrefaction, setting the stage for Pasteur's own leap into the invisible world.

segments.anchor_sentence: In 1831, thirty-two years after the magnificent Spallanzani died, microbe hunting had come to a standstill once more.

visual.scene_description: A terrified nine-year-old boy fleeing from a crowd gathered at a blacksmith shop in a small French mountain village, where a farmer is being cauterized with a red-hot iron after a mad wolf attack.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a small frightened boy in 19th-century French peasant clothing running from a stone blacksmith shop doorway, a crowd of villagers in coats and caps pressing around the entrance, orange glow of a forge visible inside, mountainous eastern French village with slate-roofed stone houses, overcast autumn sky, scattered fallen leaves on cobblestones

segments.anchor_sentence: I am not going to try to make believe that this terrible event made the nine-year-old Louis Pasteur determine to find out the cause and cure of hydrophobia some day—that would be very romantic—but it wouldn't be true.

visual.scene_description: Young Pasteur as a boy painting a portrait of his mother by a river near the family tannery, showing his early artistic temperament before he turned to science.

segments.anchor_sentence: "To will is a great thing, dear sisters," he wrote, "for Action and Work usually follow Will, and almost always Work is accompanied by Success."

visual.scene_description: Young Pasteur as a university student emerging in a daze from a chemistry lecture in Paris, captivated by the world of science, walking through the misty Latin Quarter streets.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a young man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in a formal black frock coat stepping out of a grand arched stone doorway of the Normal School in Paris, rows of glass bottles and colored chemical fluids visible through the window behind him, narrow misty cobblestone Latin Quarter street, gas lamps casting dim halos, pigeons on wet pavement

segments.anchor_sentence: Just then, as Pasteur was bending his snub nose and broad forehead over confused piles of crystals, the sub-visible living microbes were beginning to come back into serious notice, they were beginning to be thought of as important serious fellow creatures, just as useful as horses or elephants, by two lonely searchers, one in France and one in Germany.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur in his small dark laboratory, arms flung wide in triumph after discovering the mirror-image crystals of tartaric acid, with heaps of tiny crystals on the bench and a microscope nearby.
